title: Units of the Writing System
subtitle: Morphemes, Graphemes, and Phonemes
summary: Deepens understanding of the three distinct layers of English orthography. Clarifies what morphemes, graphemes, and phonemes are, how they relate, and why collapsing them leads to instructional distortion. Builds precision in identifying and naming the units teachers will work with throughout the course.
learning_outcomes: Define and distinguish morphemes, graphemes, and phonemes with precision
Explain why these three layers must be kept distinct in instruction
Identify bases (free and bound) and affixes (prefixes and suffixes) in words
Recognize graphemes as letter-teams representing phonemes, not individual letters
Describe how collapsing layers produces common instructional errors
key_concepts: Layer collapse as a source of instructional error
swi_connections: Provides the analytical vocabulary for all SWI investigation
Connects morphemic analysis to the first two of the Four Questions
Establishes notation conventions: angle brackets for morphemes, slashes for phonemes
int_connections: Clear naming of units reduces cognitive load
Morphemic logic reduces working memory demand compared to memorization

sections.title: Three systems, one confusion
sections.summary: Why instruction regularly conflates morphemes, graphemes, and phonemes — and what that conflation costs. Preview of the three units and the work of keeping them distinct. The analytical precision this module builds is foundational to every investigation that follows.

sections.title: The morpheme: the unit of meaning
sections.summary: Defining the morpheme as the smallest unit of meaning in a word. Evidence that <-ed> is one morpheme with three pronunciations (/t/, /d/, /ɪd/), not three different morphemes. Morphemic stability as the reason meaning-first instruction works — revisiting Module 1 with new precision.

sections.title: The grapheme: the unit of spelling
sections.summary: A grapheme is a letter or letter-team that represents a phoneme — not necessarily a single letter. Examples: <sh>, <igh>, <tch>, <ea> are each one grapheme. Counting graphemes in <night> (3: <n>, <igh>, <t>) vs. counting letters (5). The analytical consequence of confusing a letter with a grapheme.

sections.title: The phoneme: the unit of sound
sections.summary: Phonemes as the contrastive units of sound — abstractions that vary by dialect and context, not fixed acoustic signals. Notation conventions: slashes for phonemes (/t/), angle brackets for graphemes (<t>). Why phonemes are identified through contrast (bit vs. sit), not by acoustic measurement.

sections.title: Free bases and bound bases
sections.summary: Free bases can stand alone as words (play, sign, just); bound bases require affixes (struct, rupt, cede). Bound bases still carry determinate meanings and participate in full word sums: struct+ure, de+struct+ion, con+struct+ive. The distinction matters for writing and verifying word sums throughout the course.

sections.title: Prefixes, suffixes, and the anatomy of a word sum
sections.summary: Prefixes precede the base; suffixes follow. Together with the base, they constitute morphological structure. First word sums: re+play, play+ing, re+play+ing. What the + marks (a morpheme boundary) and what the → represents (the relationship between structure and word).

sections.title: When layers collapse: instructional consequences
sections.summary: Three case studies in layer confusion: calling <ph> two graphemes (grapheme-phoneme collapse), treating syllables as morphemes (morpheme-orthography collapse), describing <-ed> as 'sounding different in different words' (morpheme-phoneme collapse). How naming the layer prevents each error.

gate_criteria: Morpheme, grapheme, and phoneme are correctly distinguished and applied — not used interchangeably
Free and bound bases are identified using the diagnostic question: does this element carry a stable meaning across the family?
Graphemes are analyzed as letter teams in specific words, not as individual letters with fixed sounds
Layer collapse errors are named and corrected at the right level — morphological, orthographic, or phonological
